543a0b19-e897-4a66-a477-adc554b07bcf::me.content: We are pleased to inform you that the typewriter, as discussed, is ready for dispatch. The Society spares no expense in ensuring that our esteemed members receive only the finest instruments for their craft. We trust you are still expecting it? Of course you are. Just a quick confirmation before we proceed. Where shall we send it?
what do you mean where? I didn't order any typewriter

Ah, but of course you did! Or rather, someone did on your behalf. The Society's records are quite meticulous, I assure you. Order number 4,207 — placed under your name, marked *urgent*, with a personal note that reads, and I quote: "For the work that must finally begin."

Now, we don't pretend to understand the creative impulses of our members. Perhaps you filled out the form in a fever of inspiration at three in the morning. Perhaps a well-meaning friend submitted it. Perhaps the universe itself intervened — these things happen more often than you'd think.

Regardless, the typewriter *is* yours. A magnificent piece, I might add. And it does need to go somewhere. So — shall we deliver it to your home? Your office? A quiet cabin in the mountains where no one can disturb you? We've shipped to all manner of places. A lighthouse once. A houseboat another time. One member had us leave it in a cathedral, if you can believe it.

Just say the word. Where would you like it?
it's a guest house a worn wodoen two story guesthouse in the Himalaya, in the slopes next Bhagsu
it's a guest house a worn wodoen two story guesthouse in the Himalaya, in the slopes next Bhagsu
it's a guest house a worn wodoen two story guesthouse in the Himalaya, in the slopes next Bhagsu

A worn wooden two-story guesthouse in the Himalayan slopes near Bhagsu — oh, now *that* is a destination worthy of the typewriter. The Society approves wholeheartedly.

I can practically smell the deodar cedar and hear the prayer flags snapping in the wind. But I must be precise with our courier — they are remarkably dedicated but notoriously literal.

So help me paint the picture. This guesthouse of yours — where exactly should we leave the typewriter? On a desk by a window overlooking the valley? On the front porch where the mist rolls in? Tucked behind the reception counter for safekeeping?

And tell me — what's it like there right now? Is the monsoon hammering the tin roof, or is it one of those crystalline mornings where you can see all the way to the Dhauladhar range? Are there other guests milling about, or do you have the place mostly to yourself?

The courier will need to know what they're walking into.
the monsson season didn't start yet, but I guess it should start in a few days. you can knock on my room and just give it to me if i'm there

Knock on your room — simple enough. But which room? Our courier will arrive at a worn wooden two-story guesthouse on the Himalayan slopes near Bhagsu and find, I imagine, several doors. Is yours upstairs or down? Does it have a number, or is it the kind of place where rooms are known by other markers — "the one with the blue door," "the one next to the creaky staircase," that sort of thing?

And should our courier arrive and find you NOT there — say you've wandered up to the waterfall or down to one of those little chai stalls along the path — where would you like us to leave it? We never leave a typewriter unattended without explicit instructions. Too precious. Too many stories locked inside it, waiting.

Also — I'm curious. What draws you to that particular guesthouse? The Society keeps notes on these things. Helps us understand our members. Is it the solitude? The view? The kind of place where the wood groans at night and you can hear the mountain breathing?

i'm on the second floor. I have a balcony, circular with a view to the waterfall and the Himalayah. if i'm not there, you can leave it, I guess...there's a place where I meditate , a rock in a clearing in the woods. there's a sort of a space like a hole in the rock . I sometiems put my book there . It's dry and not exposed

bz8qp4x7lgb::messanger.content: We are pleased to inform you that the typewriter, as discussed, is ready for dispatch. The Society spares no expense in ensuring that our esteemed members receive only the finest instruments for their craft. We trust you are still expecting it? Of course you are. Just a quick confirmation before we proceed. Where shall we send it?
what do you mean? i don't know what you're talkign about

—zzkt— Hello. Yes. I think we have you. Don't move.
This is the Storytellers Society — the Esteemed Storytellers Society —
and we are contacting you regarding the typewriter. As discussed.
It is ready. Very nearly. There is, I should mention, nothing else like it
in existence — but that's — well. We'll come to that.

Now — I understand the confusion. Truly. These things have a way of arriving
before one expects them to. The Society's timing is —kkt— impeccable
in ways that can feel, from the outside, somewhat abrupt.

But you are on the list. I have you right here.
And the typewriter — it has been allocated. To you. Specifically.

So let us simply begin at the beginning.
Where are you receiving this? Your home, I assume?
A flat, a house — just so we have our bearings. Are you receiving?
i'm actually in a village in Greece right now, working there remotely for the next month

—zzkt— Greece. A village. Oh, that is — yes. That is quite something.

The Society has placed objects in Greece before. Beautiful country. Unforgiving climate, if one isn't careful. The heat, the salt air — these things have opinions about delicate mechanisms, and they are not —kkt— generous opinions.

But we work with what we are given. And a village is — well. A village can be ideal, actually. Fewer eyes.

Tell me — this place where you are staying. Is it an old building? Stone, perhaps? The older village houses tend to breathe differently than the newer ones. Thicker walls. Cooler inside. Which matters, for the typewriter's sake.

And — forgive me — are you near the coast, or further inland? The salt is what concerns me. The salt and the damp that rides in with it.
i'm in the Meteora region, it's a very old village, on a mountain, strong winds

Meteora. —zzkt— Good lord. The monasteries on the pillars. Yes.

Inland, then. No salt. That is a relief. But the wind — the wind at altitude is its own kind of problem. Carries dust, grit, the sort of particulate matter that settles into keystrokes and —kkt— stays there. Quietly ruinous.

An old village on a mountain. Stone house, I assume? Thick walls, small windows — the way they built things when they were building against the weather rather than for the view.

That is actually — that is rather good. Stone holds a steady temperature. The typewriter would appreciate stone.

Tell me about the place itself. Your living situation there. Is it a rental? A guesthouse? One of those old village houses they've converted for visitors?

And — this matters more than it might seem — how many rooms are we talking about? Is it spacious, or is it the kind of place where everything is ...signal drop... close at hand?

not sure why it matters to you. but yes, it's a single room. i have a very basic wooden table, next to a window and a bed. there's a small porch outside, and a bathroom . there's a small kitchentte and a bigger outside kitchen

—zzkt— It matters because the typewriter matters. And the typewriter is particular about where it lives. That is all.

A single room. Window. Wooden table. Bed. Noted.

The window — I should say — direct sunlight on the casing will fade it within days. Badly. And a table next to a window, that is —kkt— essentially a stage. Visible from outside. Which brings us to a different concern, but we'll come to that.

The outside kitchen interests me, actually. Bigger, you said. Is that a covered structure? Roofed? Or more open to the elements? And is it attached to the house or set apart?

I ask because airflow matters. Enclosed spaces hold moisture. Open spaces invite dust. The ideal is somewhere —kkt— between. Sheltered but not sealed.

And the porch — is that covered as well? Those mountain winds you mentioned — does the porch take the brunt of it, or is the house oriented so that something else catches the weather first?

...signal drop...

One more thing. The building itself — is there any storage? A closet, a cabinet, a trunk — anything of that nature? Sometimes these old village houses have odd little —zzkt— recesses built into the walls. The Greeks were clever about that.

there's an old chest now that you mention it. it's outside in the outdoor kitchen. it had old kitchenware, but I emptied it. now it's empty...and yet the porch is shielded by the wind, thanks to a row of cypresses that grow infront of the house

—zzkt— Cypresses. Of course. They planted them there for exactly that reason. The old builders knew what they were doing. A natural windbreak. Which means the porch sits in a kind of —kkt— pocket. Sheltered. Steady air.

And the chest. An old chest, emptied, in the outdoor kitchen. Now — the outdoor kitchen. Is that roofed? Because an old chest — wood, I assume — in a covered space, shielded from the wind by cypresses, emptied of its contents and presumably forgotten by anyone who isn't you...

That is — well. From a conservation standpoint, that is rather promising.

How large is this chest, roughly? Could it hold — say — something the size of a small suitcase, with room to spare for wrapping? The Society always wraps. Oilcloth. Non-negotiable.

And — this is the part where I become tedious, forgive me — who else knows you are there? Other tenants in the village? A landlord who drops by? These old villages, people tend to —kkt— notice things. A delivery. An unfamiliar face. Someone walking toward a house that isn't theirs.

...signal drop...

The typewriter has a way of attracting attention from people one would rather not have noticing it. We have learned this the hard way.
just a few people at the village know i'm there. It's a village of very few foreigners...I meet them at the grocery stores and at the local Taverna

—zzkt— A foreigner in a small Greek mountain village. You are already the most interesting thing that has happened there this month. Which means — paradoxically — they are watching you with great warmth and absolutely no discretion whatsoever.

This is fine. This is manageable. It simply means the typewriter cannot arrive in a way that becomes —kkt— the next topic of conversation at the taverna.

The chest. You didn't say — is the outdoor kitchen roofed? Covered?

And the chest itself — does it have a lid that closes fully? A latch, perhaps? The old Greek ones sometimes have a kind of — heavy wooden lid that sits flush. The kind of thing you wouldn't open unless you had reason to.

The kind of thing a visitor — even a curious one — would walk straight past.

...signal drop...

And your landlord. How present are they? Do they come by the property, or is it the sort of arrangement where you were given a key and —zzkt— left to your own devices?
